19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

George Herbert Mead

1863 – 1931

Self, gesture, and mind as a social process.

George Herbert Mead’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a social, embodied, temporal, and non-reductive naturalism. Mind is not a private substance inside the head. Consciousness is not the foundational inner theater from which society is later inferred. The “soul,” insofar as Mead takes the older religious and metaphysical vocabulary seriously, is not a disembodied immortal entity but the historically transformed problem of the social self: a self that seeks continuity, recognition, salvation, and a universal community adequate to its values.

The compressed thesis is this: mind arises when an organism, through significant symbols, can call out in itself the responses it calls out in others, thereby becoming an object to itself within a social act. Human consciousness, in its distinctive form, is therefore reflexive social participation. The self is not born ready-made; it emerges through language, role-taking, and the generalized other. The “I” supplies novelty, initiative, and unpredictability; the “Me” is the organized set of social attitudes internalized by the individual. The theological dimension matters because Mead’s account of selfhood, universality, value, and “salvation” secularizes but does not simply discard Christian motifs of universal community, divine intelligibility, and the destiny of the soul. Mead’s father was a Congregational minister, his mother was deeply religious and later president of Mount Holyoke, and Mead himself moved toward naturalism and non-belief while retaining a serious concern with the moral and social meaning of religious categories.

One interpretive caution is essential. Mind, Self, and Society is the most famous source, but it is a posthumous 1934 text edited from lecture materials by Charles W. Morris; a rigorous reconstruction should therefore triangulate it with Mead’s essays such as “The Social Self,” “The Mechanism of Social Consciousness,” “The Genesis of the Self and Social Control,” “The Objective Reality of Perspectives,” The Philosophy of the Present, and The Philosophy of the Act. The Mead Project table of contents shows the familiar organization into “Mind,” “Self,” and “Society,” but the posthumous status of the book is also emphasized in German reception, which warns against treating it as a polished monograph written by Mead himself.

A further philological warning matters for the soul/mind/spirit question. In German, Mind, Self and Society is received as Geist, Identität und Gesellschaft; in French as L’Esprit, le soi et la société; in Spanish as Espíritu, persona y sociedad; and in Italian as Mente, Sé e società. These translations pull “mind” toward Geist, esprit, and espíritu—terms that can also mean spirit—whereas the Italian mente stays closer to the English psychological sense. This is not a trivial translation issue: Mead’s “mind” is neither a Cartesian mental substance nor merely “brain,” and the wider European vocabulary often makes visible the spiritual and theological resonances that English-language sociology sometimes flattens.

⸻

1. Mead’s starting point: not inner consciousness, but the social act

Mead begins neither with a private ego nor with bare neural events, but with the act. Organisms are already involved in environments. They respond, inhibit responses, complete acts, fail, adjust, and coordinate with other organisms. The mind is to be explained within this field of conduct. In Mind, Self, and Society, Mead criticizes approaches that begin with “states of consciousness” and then try to connect them to bodily behavior through psychophysical parallelism; he wants consciousness explained from action rather than action explained by hidden mental states.

This is why Mead calls his method “social behaviorism,” but the phrase is easily misunderstood. Mead is not Watson’s reductive behaviorist. He accepts that mind must be studied through conduct, but he rejects the idea that thought can be reduced to mere muscular, verbal, or conditioned reflex behavior. His disagreement with Watson is explicit: Watson rightly shifts attention from introspection to behavior, but he cannot adequately explain the social and symbolic structure of thought.

The basic unit is the social act, not the isolated individual. A gesture by one organism becomes a stimulus to another organism’s response; that response in turn becomes a stimulus back to the first. This “conversation of gestures” exists before reflective consciousness. Dogs fighting, animals mating, insects coordinating, and humans cooperating all involve social processes, but not all involve mind in the full human sense. The decisive development occurs when the gesture becomes a significant symbol: a gesture that calls out in the actor the same type of response it calls out in others.

Vocal gesture is especially important because the speaker hears herself as others hear her. A cry, word, command, or question can affect the speaker and the hearer in comparable ways. This is the natural mechanism by which the individual can “take the attitude of the other.” Mead therefore makes language central, not as a detached system of signs, but as a social-physiological mechanism through which conduct becomes reflexive.

French reception captures this well. A 1963 Revue française de sociologie review of L’Esprit, le Soi et la Société states that, for Mead, human conduct differs from animal conduct because the human being can provoke in himself the same stimulus and response that he provokes in another; human language is therefore irreducible to animal “language” because the verbal gesture has the same meaning for speaker and addressee.

⸻

2. Meaning and ideas: Mead’s anti-representational semantics

For Mead, meaning is not first a private idea in the head that is later attached to a word. Meaning is a relation within conduct: the relation between a gesture, the response it calls out, and the completion of the act. A footprint “means” a bear because it calls out in the organism a set of possible responses toward a future or absent object. In this sense, meaning is anticipatory and practical before it is contemplative.

An “idea” is therefore not a little mental picture. It is an organized set of possible responses. When one says “chair,” “danger,” “law,” “God,” “friend,” or “enemy,” the significant symbol organizes attitudes and future conduct. Thought is the internalized conversation of such gestures. The individual speaks to herself from the standpoint of another, then from the standpoint of the group, then from the standpoint of an increasingly generalized community.

This gives Mead a powerful philosophy of intentionality. Consciousness is “about” objects because organisms are already acting toward possible completions of conduct. Objects are not passively copied by the mind; they are constituted as objects within fields of possible manipulation, avoidance, consummation, communication, and shared response. This does not make Mead a subjective idealist. The object is not invented by private consciousness; it is stabilized through action, resistance, communication, and the organized perspectives of a community.

Portuguese reception is helpful here. José Antônio Damásio Abib describes Mead’s theory as social, dialogical, and anti-representational: communicative-participatory action, involving shared meanings, is the social basis for language, mind, and self. The “I” is not only biological or impulsive but also observational, cognitive, critical, and capable of social change; cognition develops through impulse-control, reflective deliberation, and moral judgment.

⸻

3. Mind: not a thing, but reflective social intelligence

Mead’s “mind” is best defined as the reflective, symbolically mediated organization of conduct in which an organism can indicate to itself and to others the possible meanings, responses, and consequences involved in an act. Mind appears where action is delayed, inhibited, and reorganized. If an organism simply acts immediately, there may be intelligent adjustment, but not reflective mind in the full sense. When conflicting tendencies check immediate action, the organism can hold alternative completions before itself; this field of possible responses is the field in which mind operates.

This delayed-response structure is crucial. Mind is not merely language, but language gives delayed conduct a social and objective form. Through significant symbols, one can say to oneself, “Do not do that,” “What will they think?”, “What would be fair?”, “What happens if I choose otherwise?” The individual thereby becomes both actor and audience, speaker and hearer, initiator and critic. Thought is an internal forum. Mead even uses St. Paul’s discussion of speaking in tongues to clarify the point: vocalization is not meaningful merely because sound is made; it must be translatable into significant speech that calls out an intelligible response.

In The Philosophy of the Act, Mead radicalizes the thesis: “the locus of mind is not in the individual.” That phrase should not be read as denying brains, bodies, or individual experience. It means that the mental process is a phase of a wider act involving organism, environment, objects, symbols, and other selves. Private imagery may be inaccessible to others in practice, but its privacy is not evidence of a separate mental substance.

This places Mead close to later enactive, embodied, and socially externalist philosophies of mind, though those labels are anachronistic if imposed too heavily. The mind is not sealed inside the skull, but neither is it a mystical group mind floating above individuals. It is enacted in conduct, distributed across symbolic interaction, and made possible by biological organisms capable of delayed response, vocal self-stimulation, manipulation of objects, and role-taking.

⸻

4. Consciousness and self-consciousness

Mead distinguishes, sometimes imperfectly but importantly, between consciousness and self-consciousness. There can be immediate experience, sensation, affect, and intelligent adjustment without a self. The organism can operate, perceive, avoid, consume, manipulate, and even behave intelligently without being an object to itself. Self-consciousness is a further development. It arises when the organism can take the attitude of others toward itself.

The self is therefore not present at birth. The child is not first a private ego that later enters society. Rather, the child becomes a self by entering social processes in which gestures, play, games, roles, rules, and language allow the child to see herself from the standpoint of others. The body is there before the self, but the body as “my body,” as an object within my reflective experience, is socially mediated.

Mead’s most direct statement is that mind is a social phenomenon. It arises only when the social process as a whole enters into the experience of an individual. The individual can then address herself, judge herself, command herself, excuse herself, and reconstruct herself. Consciousness, in its distinctively human form, is reflexive participation in a social process.

This is why Mead does not make introspection foundational. Introspection is itself a social achievement. To “look within” is to take toward oneself attitudes that have first been learned in communication with others. Inner consciousness is “socially organized” by importing into the individual the structure of outer social relations.

Spanish-language interpretation brings out the anti-Cartesian point. A Universidad Autónoma del Estado de Hidalgo essay on Mead says that communication allows the person to speak to herself and answer herself as if speaking with another; it then explicitly says that Mead’s symbolic and social account leaves aside a “filosofía de la conciencia” centered on the isolated individual consciousness.

⸻

5. The self as “soul” transformed

Mead rarely gives us a metaphysics of “soul” in the traditional theological sense. When he discusses the older language of soul, double, or hidden subject, he usually translates it into the problem of the self. In Mind, Self, and Society, he notes that primitive thought can imagine a “double” that leaves the body in sleep or death; this is an important anthropological datum, but not Mead’s ontology.

The deeper philosophical issue is that the acting “I” cannot be caught as an object in the moment of action. I can remember what I did; I can take responsibility for what I did; I can interpret my action after the fact. But the present “I” as response is never fully before consciousness. In “The Mechanism of Social Consciousness,” Mead connects this elusive “I” with the Kantian transcendental self and with William James’s idea of a soul “behind the scenes,” but he does not endorse a hidden soul-substance. He treats this “I” as a functional feature of action: the subject of response cannot be objectified except retrospectively, in the “Me.”

This gives us Mead’s transformation of the soul. The soul is not a metaphysical particle inhabiting the body. It is the felt and conceptual problem of selfhood: the fact that the self is both object and subject, both socially formed and creatively responsive, both visible as “Me” and elusive as “I.” The traditional soul names, in mythic or theological form, the unity, continuity, responsibility, and hidden initiative of the person. Mead reconstructs these features without positing a separable spiritual substance.

The self, then, is not reducible to the “Me.” If it were, Mead would be a social determinist. Nor is the self reducible to the “I.” If it were, he would be a romantic individualist. The self is the dynamic process in which the “I” responds to the organized attitudes of the “Me,” and that response can transform the social order whose attitudes it has inherited.

German summaries often state the distinction plainly: the “Me” is the internalized social order of roles, norms, and expectations, while the “I” is the creative and spontaneous side that responds to and can question those expectations. That is accurate as a first approximation, provided one does not detach the “I” from the social process altogether.

⸻

6. The “I,” the “Me,” freedom, and novelty

The “Me” is the organized set of attitudes of others that the individual takes toward herself. It is the internalized community, including its language, norms, roles, and generalized expectations. The “I” is the response to that organized field. It is not directly predictable because it is the actual emergence of conduct in the present. The “I” gives the self novelty, initiative, and a sense of freedom; the “Me” gives it structure, intelligibility, and social accountability.

Mead’s account of freedom is not libertarian in the metaphysical sense of an uncaused will. Freedom is emergent response within a structured situation. The “I” is not outside nature or society, but it is not mechanically deducible from the “Me.” In action, something new may enter the social process. This is how the individual can become a source of reconstruction rather than merely a bearer of social order.

French reception has developed this point in relation to democracy and universal society. A 2021 OpenEdition essay argues that Mead contains two models of self-consciousness: one tied to problematic situations and one tied to intersubjective social exchange. On the second model, a more universal society does not necessarily annihilate self-consciousness; it can deepen it by multiplying the roles and perspectives from which one can address oneself. The article cites Mead’s formula that “we must be others to be ourselves,” a useful condensation of Mead’s philosophy of selfhood.

This matters for the philosophy of mind because it prevents two errors. First, the self is not an isolated consciousness. Second, the social self is not a conformist automaton. The self is a site where inherited social meanings can be re-enacted, resisted, reorganized, and universalized.

⸻

7. Embodiment: brain, hand, voice, and environment

Mead is not an idealist who treats mind as pure discourse. He repeatedly emphasizes the organism, the nervous system, delayed response, the hand, manipulation, and the vocal gesture. Human intelligence flowers because the body can inhibit action, manipulate objects, and use signs that affect the speaker as well as the hearer.

The hand matters because physical things become objects in a manipulatory field. The voice matters because it returns to the speaker. The nervous system matters because it makes delayed and alternative responses possible. The community matters because symbols are significant only within shared acts. Mead’s philosophy of mind is therefore neither brain-only nor society-only; it is an organism-environment-symbol-community theory.

Italian reception makes this embodied-social point vivid. A review titled “Linguaggio e autocoscienza” says that Mead offers a theory of intelligence explaining the emergence of mind, self-consciousness, and self from the social act and the mechanism of gesture. It emphasizes the special role of the vocal gesture: the voice reaches both addressee and speaker, almost like something returning from outside.

The same Italian review also stresses the temporal dimension: to have mind is to have time, in the sense of memory and anticipation. Through language, individuals indicate future responses to objects; self-consciousness means objectifying oneself and seeing oneself from the standpoint of others or the generalized other. The reflective self is therefore not private but common and relational.

⸻

8. Temporality: the mind lives in the delayed act

Mead’s theory of mind cannot be separated from time. Mind appears when immediate response is inhibited and possible completions of action are held in suspense. Reflection is the pause in which the organism can compare alternative futures, reconstruct the meaning of the past, and select a course of conduct. Memory and foresight are not ornaments added to mind; they are constitutive of mind.

This also explains why the “I” is never fully present as object. The “I” appears retrospectively: I discover what I did, what I meant, what I became, by remembering my response after it has entered the field of the “Me.” The self is therefore historical. It is always reconstructing its own past from the standpoint of a present problem and a possible future.

In The Philosophy of the Present, Mead argues that reality is located in the present, but not in a punctual instant. The present is a functional field in which pasts and futures are reconstructed in relation to ongoing conduct. This has direct consequences for mind: consciousness is not a timeless container of experiences, but the living reorganization of past and future possibilities within an emergent present.

⸻

9. Objectivity and perspectives

Mead’s social theory of mind is not subjectivism. He develops an “objective reality of perspectives.” A perspective is not merely a visual viewpoint or a private impression; it is a way an organism’s activity stratifies its environment. A fish, a snail, a human child, a scientist, and a political community inhabit different objective perspectives because they act within different organism-environment relations.

Human objectivity arises when the individual can enter the perspectives of others and especially of the group. The social scientist’s data, for Mead, include experiences in which individuals take the attitude of the community. A more universal community can transform narrower perspectives, but this is not simple majority rule. It is the reconstruction of perspectives through communication, experiment, and shared conditions of intelligibility.

This gives Mead a distinctive answer to the problem of objectivity in philosophy of mind. The mind does not escape subjectivity by jumping into a God’s-eye view. It achieves objectivity by taking increasingly organized, testable, and universalizable perspectives. Science is an advanced form of this social process, not because scientists have no perspective, but because experimental method coordinates perspectives under common conditions.

⸻

10. Theology: not a distraction, but a structural analogue

The theological material is not incidental. Mead’s naturalism is post-theological, not simply anti-theological. He sees Christianity as historically significant because it articulated the idea of a universal society in which the interest of each could be understood in relation to the interest of all. He also argues that the Christian picture of divine intelligence and natural law helped make the world intelligible as an ordered whole; modern science inherits and transforms that structure of intelligibility.

Science, for Mead, does not merely destroy religion. It partly occupies the place once held by the religiously interpreted universe: it gives human beings an intelligible world in which the mind can feel “at home.” But science also creates a problem because it does not by itself state the ends of conduct. It gives conditions, means, and explanatory control, but the statement of values and ends still belongs to moral and social life.

This is where Mead’s remarks on salvation become philosophically important. The religious problem is not simply the fate of a private soul after death. Mead says the self is a social entity, and the religious demand for salvation concerns the social self that is “not at home.” Religion addresses the need for a social order in which the self’s values can be realized. Religious experience has value insofar as it contributes to social development and the reconstruction of community.

Mead’s treatment of immortality is especially revealing. He does not defend traditional personal immortality as a metaphysical doctrine. He reframes immortality as the continued existence of social value recognized as belonging to an individual. A person’s “immortality,” in this naturalized sense, is the persistence of the value embodied in that person within a continuing social order.

His comments on Jesus also fit the theory of mind. Jesus represents, for Mead, the embodiment of a universal human community: all as members of one family, all standing in a relation of mutual recognition. Salvation means finding oneself in that attitude toward oneself and others. This is a theological form of the generalized other, but raised to the level of a universal community of value.

The theological significance of Mead’s philosophy of mind is therefore fourfold. First, the “soul” becomes the socially formed self seeking adequate recognition and continuity. Second, God’s-eye intelligibility is transformed into scientific and communal objectivity. Third, salvation becomes social reconstruction rather than private escape. Fourth, universal religion becomes an anticipatory form of the generalized other: a community in which the self can take the attitudes of all toward all.

⸻

11. Non-English reception: what it adds

German reception is especially useful for resisting a purely individualist reading. German discussions emphasize Geist, Identität, and Gesellschaft: mind/spirit, identity, and society belong together. A Springer chapter by Benjamin Jörissen highlights Mead’s Protestant background, his distance from inherited dogma, and the early problem of what “subject” and “soul” could mean under modern conditions. SozTheo’s summary states the standard German reading: identity emerges through interaction, not isolated consciousness; the “Me” represents internalized social order, while the “I” is the creative response capable of questioning it.

French reception often sharpens the social and linguistic mechanism. The 1963 Persée review stresses that Mead’s social behaviorism is not Watsonian behaviorism; the crucial human capacity is self-stimulation through symbols, delayed response, and internalization of the other’s conduct. The 2021 OpenEdition debate adds a more political-philosophical question: does universal society diminish individuality, or deepen self-consciousness by expanding the range of perspectives one can internalize?

Spanish reception, at least in the UAEH essay cited here, is valuable because it frames Mead against the “philosophy of consciousness.” The point is not merely that society influences consciousness, but that the very model of beginning from isolated individual consciousness is inadequate. Communication, gesture, and the generalized other are the conditions under which the person becomes intelligible to herself.

Portuguese reception, represented by Abib’s article, is useful for connecting Mead to a dialogical and anti-representational theory of the subject. It explicitly reads the “I” as cognitive, critical, and capable of social transformation, not merely as biological impulse. That reading helps correct oversimplified accounts in which Mead’s “I” becomes either raw spontaneity or a mysterious leftover from individualism.

Italian reception, in the review from Quaderni di Cultura Junghiana, is especially helpful for the soul/mind/consciousness question because it stresses language, self-consciousness, and alterity. It says that at the heart of the “I,” the word, and self-consciousness is the relation with the other; one cannot speak or become oneself alone. That is a concise formulation of Mead’s deepest anti-Cartesian claim.

⸻

12. A systematic Mead-inspired philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Mead would have the following core theses.

First, naturalism is correct, but reductive materialism is inadequate. Mind arises in nature, but nature includes emergent social, symbolic, and perspectival structures. The mental is not a supernatural substance, but neither is it merely neural machinery.

Second, the social act is ontologically prior to the reflective individual self. Mead does not deny individuals; he denies that the self-enclosed individual ego is the starting point. Social processes can exist before self-conscious individuals, and self-conscious individuals emerge within such processes.

Third, meaning is practical and relational. A meaning is an organized relation between gesture, response, object, and possible completion of action. Words mean because they coordinate conduct and call out shared responses.

Fourth, mind is reflective conduct mediated by significant symbols. It is the internalization of social communication, not a private container of representations.

Fifth, consciousness is not all-or-nothing. There is immediate experience and intelligent adjustment before self-consciousness. Distinctively human self-consciousness arises when the organism becomes an object to itself by taking the attitude of others.

Sixth, the self is a process, not a substance. It is the ongoing relation between “I” and “Me,” between creative response and organized social attitudes.

Seventh, freedom is emergent social novelty. The “I” is not an uncaused metaphysical will, but the unpredictable present response through which new conduct and new social meanings can arise.

Eighth, objectivity is social-perspectival, not view-from-nowhere. Objectivity is achieved through the organization, testing, and universalization of perspectives.

Ninth, the body is indispensable. Mead’s mind requires nervous inhibition, vocal self-stimulation, gesture, manipulation, and organism-environment interaction. His social theory is embodied, not merely linguistic.

Tenth, the theological “soul” is reconstructed as the value-bearing social self. Mead rejects the soul as ghostly substance, but he preserves the seriousness of the soul-problem: continuity, responsibility, salvation, universality, and the relation of the personal self to an intelligible and morally adequate world.

⸻

13. Tensions and unresolved problems

Mead’s theory leaves several difficult questions.

The first is the problem of phenomenal consciousness. Mead gives a powerful account of self-consciousness, meaning, and reflective thought, but he does not offer a detailed theory of raw phenomenal feel in the contemporary sense. Pain, sensation, affect, and mood can be accommodated in his framework as phases of organism-environment conduct, but he does not make them central.

The second is the problem of language dependence. Mead sometimes seems to make self-consciousness depend heavily on language, especially vocal language. Yet gesture, embodiment, non-vocal communication, and atypical developmental cases complicate that picture. A Mead-inspired theory should probably say that significant symbolization, not vocal speech alone, is necessary for full self-consciousness.

The third is the problem of social conformity. If the self arises through the attitudes of others, can it resist the community? Mead’s “I” is designed to solve this problem, but the exact status of the “I” remains ambiguous: biological impulse, emergent novelty, social creativity, or all three. French and Portuguese readings rightly stress that the “I” must be interpreted as socially mediated, not as a pre-social soul smuggled back into the system.

The fourth is the problem of the universal community. Mead’s religious and democratic universalism is attractive because it expands recognition, but it risks suppressing difference if “universal” means one homogeneous set of meanings. The better Mead-inspired interpretation is that universal society deepens self-consciousness by multiplying translatable perspectives, not by erasing plurality.

The fifth is the problem of theological residue. Mead is a naturalist and not a traditional theologian. Yet his concepts of universal community, salvation, immortality-as-social-value, and the mind at home in an intelligible universe retain theological force. A secular reading that ignores this residue misses part of the architecture of his thought.

⸻

Final synthesis

Mead’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a social naturalist transfiguration of the soul. The old soul was imagined as an inner substance, hidden behind bodily life, responsible for personal identity, moral accountability, and ultimate destiny. Mead dissolves that substance, but he does not dissolve the problems it named. He relocates them.

Personal identity becomes the self’s ongoing conversation between “I” and “Me.” Moral accountability becomes the individual’s capacity to take the attitudes of others and the generalized other. Inner life becomes the internalized conversation of significant symbols. Freedom becomes emergent response within the social act. Objectivity becomes the organization of perspectives in a universalizable community of discourse. Salvation becomes the reconstruction of social life so that the self can be at home in a world of realized values. Immortality becomes the continuation of social value embodied in a person.

So the comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Mead is not merely “symbolic interactionism.” It is a full account of mind as embodied symbolic sociality, consciousness as reflexive role-taking, selfhood as temporally emergent dialogue, objectivity as organized perspective-taking, and soul as the value-bearing social self seeking universal recognition and continuity.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
